context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We donâ€™t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%â€”fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; weâ€™ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the studentâ€™s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 studentsâ€”larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semestersâ€™ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friendâ€™s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 2

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Tuesday 20th February, 2024
Days Covered: 03 and 04 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Outlining Proofs [10 points]

See lecture 02 (or the corresponding lecture notes) for a discussion of outlining proofs, even with only partial information. Practice this skill by outlining proofs for each of the "theorems" below as much as you can, using the techniques indicated. Pretend that the underlined words are meaningful but that their precise meanings are not available here. This means your "proofs" will have missing steps that are impossible to complete without definitions for these words, but that's fineâ€”we're interested in the parts of the proof that can be completed even without knowing or considering the underlined terms' meanings. You should break down the proof as granularly as you can using just the given information, and at any step that needs additional information, identify the remaining task or tasks using square brackets. The example seen in lecture looks like this:

    Theorem: "For every integer n, the number n is fooish precisely when n + 1 is barsome."
    Proof Outline. Suppose n is any integer; we must show F(n) and B(n + 1) both imply each other.
    To prove F(n) IMPLIES B(n + 1), first assume F(n) is true. [TODO: prove B(n + 1).]
    To prove B(n + 1) IMPLIES F(n), instead assume B(n + 1) is true. [TODO: prove F(n).]

(a) [3 pts] Theorem: "Every natural number is tall or wide, but not both." (Proof method: proof by cases.)
(b) [3 pts] Theorem: "There exists a happy natural number between 10 and 1000 that has no happy proper divisors." (Proof method to use: choose 25 as the example.)
(c) [4 pts] Theorem: "There are no devious natural numbers." (Proof method to use: regular induction, with base cases 0 and 1.)
    Hint: Be sure to clearly identify your predicate P(n), and to precisely specify any assumptions and/or goals in your base cases and inductive step.


Problem 2. Spotting Errors [15 points]

Define a sequence of integers (x1, x2, x3, ...) as follows: x1 = 4, x2 = 13, and for all i â‰¥ 3, xi = 6Â·x_{iâˆ’1} âˆ’ 8Â·x_{iâˆ’2}. So the sequence begins with (x1, x2, x3, x4, ...) = (4, 13, 46, 172, ...). Our goal is to prove that for all i â‰¥ 1, xi > 0.

Addled Abra tries to prove the claim directly by strong induction with inductive hypothesis P(i) := "xi > 0", but gets stuck in the inductive step: xi+1 = 6Â·xi âˆ’ 8Â·x_{iâˆ’1} > 0 âˆ’ 8Â·x_{iâˆ’1}, "but, uh, ... Huh, I don't think this is working." Abra gets points for honesty, and goes to the instructors for help.

(a) [3 pts] Befuddled Brynmor tries to help by proving the claim using the stronger induction hypothesis Q(i) := "xi â‰¥ 2^i", via strong induction. His inductive step plugs xi â‰¥ 2^i and x_{iâˆ’1} â‰¥ 2^{iâˆ’1} into xi+1 = 6Â·xi âˆ’ 8Â·x_{iâˆ’1} to get xi+1 â‰¥ 6Â·2^i âˆ’ 8Â·2^{iâˆ’1} = 4Â·2^{iâˆ’1} = 2^{i+1}. What is his mistake?

(b) [3 pts] Erroneous Erik tries to help by proving the claim using (regular) induction on the stronger hypothesis R(i) := "xi â‰¥ 3Â·x_{iâˆ’1}", for every i â‰¥ 2, with base case R(2): x2 = 13 â‰¥ 12 = 3Â·x1. What is his mistake?

(c) [3 pts] Overzealous Zach tries to help by proving the claim using strong induction on the stronger hypothesis S(i) := "xi = 4^i", for every i â‰¥ 1, with base case S(1): x1 = 4 = 4^1. What is his mistake?
    Hint: Zach's claim is false if i = 2. If you know the base case i = 1, what part of the inductive step when i = 1 fails?

(d) [6 pts] Prove the original claim that for all i â‰¥ 1, xi > 0.
    Hint: See if you can fix Erroneous Erik's argument. What stronger inductive hypothesis will you use?


Problem 3. Integer Multiplication from Bit Shifts [17 points]

Multiplying and dividing an integer n by 2 requires only a one-bit left or right shift of the binary representation of n, a fast, hardware-supported operation on most computers. For example, 6 = (0110) in binary; multiplying by 2 yields 12 = (1100). And 18 = (10010); dividing by 2 yields 9 = (01001).

We will show that the following state machine computes the product of two nonnegative integers x and y using just these shift operations, along with integer addition:
- The set of states is â„•^3 = {(r, s, a) | r, s, a âˆˆ â„•}.
- The start state is (x, y, 0).
- The transitions are:
    (r, s, a) â†’  (2r, s/2, a)            for even s > 0,
    (r, s, a) â†’  (2r, (s âˆ’ 1)/2, a + r)  for odd s > 0.
- No transitions are defined starting from triples in which s = 0.

We will show that this state machine eventually reaches a final state (the algorithm doesn't run forever), and that this final state (rf, sf, af) has af = x Â· y.

(a) [2 pts] Compute the full sequence of transitions from starting state (5, 22, 0). Does it correctly compute 5 Â· 22 = 110?
(b) [4 pts] Invariant: Define P(r, s, a) := "rs + a = xy". Prove that P is an invariant of the state machine.
(c) [1 pt] Final states: A final state is one from which no transitions are possible. Which states are final in this state machine?
(d) [3 pts] Partial correctness: Use Parts (b) and (c) to conclude that, in any reachable final state (rf, sf, af), af must be equal to the product xy.

Now we consider termination. For a natural number n, let bitcount(n) denote the number of bits used when n is written in binary. For example, bitcount(5) = bitcount(101_2) = 3, bitcount(1) = 1, and bitcount(0) = 0. (Note: this can be written explicitly as bitcount(n) = âŒˆlog2(n + 1)âŒ‰.)

(e) [5 pts] Termination: Prove that bitcount(s) is a strictly decreasing derived variable. Explain why this implies that the algorithm always terminates, i.e., it has no infinite executions.
(f) [2 pts] Time bound: Explain why Part (e) implies that, from any starting state (x, y, 0), the algorithm terminates after at most bitcount(y) = âŒˆlog2(y + 1)âŒ‰ steps.


Problem 4. Invariant Enlightenment [8 points]

The ideal 6.1200 classroom has assigned desks (each student sits at the same position every day) arranged in a square grid. Students are working together to reach Invariant Enlightenment: a profound understanding of invariants so thorough that the knowledge radiates around them in a glowing aura. Invariant Enlightenment never goes away (it's an invariant, after all!) and can sometimes be taught to other students.

[Figure: a 6Ã—6 grid of seats. The locations of students who have initially been enlightened are marked with an asterisk, scattered across the grid.]

Every day, students talk to their neighbors about invariants in an attempt to reach enlightenment. On each day, a student is enlightened if either:
- the student was already enlightened on a previous day, or
- the student was adjacent to at least two students who were enlightened on an earlier day (these neighbors worked together to teach the student all about invariants).

Here "adjacent" means the students' individual squares share an edge (front, back, left, or right); they are not adjacent if they only share a corner point. So each student is adjacent to 2, 3, or 4 others. [In the example, enlightenment spreads over successive days until all students are enlightened.]

Theorem. If fewer than n students among those in an n Ã— n arrangement are initially enlightened, and additional students reach enlightenment only by being taught by two or more neighbors, then there will be at least one student who never reaches enlightenment.

Prove this theorem.
    Hint: You'll want to find an applicable preserved state predicate or monotonic derived variable that can help you argue about the set of enlightened students as time proceeds. Make sure to prove your claims. If you are stuck, ask at Office Hours or on Piazza for an extremely powerful one-word clue (and even more hints as necessary).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, Iâ€™m stuck on Problem 2 rn, esp the induction mistakes parts. i tried reading Brynmor/Erik/Zach but idk exactly what each proof is doing wrong.
exchanges.tutor: Let's take them one at a time. Start with Brynmor (part a). Can you walk me through what his inductive step is actually claiming, and what kind of induction he says he's using?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about all three parts at once without showing any work or specific thinking. I should focus them on one part at a time and use the Socratic method to get them to identify the error themselves rather than explaining it to them. I'll start with part (a) since it's first, and ask them what they think Brynmor's proof is actually doing before pointing to any error.

exchanges.student: I think heâ€™s using strong induction on \(Q(i): x_i \ge 2^i\). In the step he assumes earlier cases are true and then plugs bounds for \(x_i\) and \(x_{i-1}\) into \(x_{i+1}=6x_i-8x_{i-1}\), but Iâ€™m not sure why thatâ€™s illegal.
exchanges.tutor: Good summary. Now think carefully: the recurrence uses x_i and x_{i-1} to compute x_{i+1}. When i=1 in the inductive step, what values does Brynmor need to have already verified?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified what Brynmor is doing but hasn't spotted the error yet. The key issue is that strong induction requires base cases for ALL values needed in the inductive step. Brynmor's recurrence uses two previous terms, so he needs two base cases, but he only checks one. I'll guide the student toward thinking about what base cases strong induction requires here without giving it away.

exchanges.student: oh wait, for \(i=1\) heâ€™d need both \(x_1\) and \(x_0\), right? but \(x_0\) isnâ€™t even defined, so I think his induction step canâ€™t start there.
exchanges.tutor: You're close! Think about it in terms of base cases specifically. Strong induction on Q(i) for iâ‰¥1 means the inductive step for proving Q(2) needs to use Q(1) and Q(0). What's the problem with Q(0) in the context of this proof?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but slightly off - the issue isn't about x_0 being undefined, it's about missing base cases. When i=1 in the inductive step, he's trying to prove Q(2), which needs Q(1) and Q(0) - but Q(0) doesn't exist since the sequence starts at x_1. The student is close but I want them to articulate this more precisely in terms of what base cases are missing rather than just 'x_0 isn't defined'.

exchanges.student: yeah, thatâ€™s the issue: \(Q(0)\) isnâ€™t part of the statement at all since the sequence starts at \(i\ge1\), and \(x_0\) isnâ€™t defined. so he doesnâ€™t have enough base cases to justify the first inductive step.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. He's missing a necessary base case that can't even be established. Good. Now move to Erik (part b) â€” what is his inductive hypothesis R(i) claiming, and what kind of induction is he using?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the error in Brynmor's proof. They understand that Q(0) is needed for the first inductive step but isn't established as a base case (and can't be, since x_0 is undefined). This is a solid answer for part (a). I should affirm this and move them to part (b) with Erik.

exchanges.student: I think Erik uses regular induction on \(R(i): x_i \ge 3x_{i-1}\) for \(i\ge2\). He has the base case \(R(2)\), but Iâ€™m confused how regular induction would prove \(R(i+1)\) when the recurrence also needs \(x_{i-1}\).
exchanges.tutor: You're onto something. Regular induction only assumes R(i) is true. But to prove R(i+1), i.e. x_{i+1} â‰¥ 3x_i, what do you actually need to know about x_{i-1} â€” and does regular induction give you that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified something important - the recurrence needs x_{i-1} in the inductive step, but regular induction only gives you R(i), not R(i-1). This is actually the key error. I should prompt them to think about what regular induction gives you versus what you actually need to prove R(i+1).

exchanges.student: I think Iâ€™d need some relation between \(x_i\) and \(x_{i-1}\), like \(x_i \ge 3x_{i-1}\), when proving \(x_{i+1}=6x_i-8x_{i-1} \ge 3x_i\). regular induction on just \(R(i)\) gives me that, wait lol now Iâ€™m mixed up again.
exchanges.tutor: Let's slow down. Forget the algebra for a second. In the inductive step, Erik wants to show x_{i+1} â‰¥ 3x_i using R(i). He'll need to bound x_{i-1}. From R(i), he gets x_{i-1} â‰¤ x_i/3. But is there a hidden assumption he's making about x_{i-1} to use that bound correctly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting confused. Let me help them think more carefully. Regular induction on R(i) for iâ‰¥2 means: assume R(i) is true, prove R(i+1). R(i) says x_i â‰¥ 3x_{i-1}. R(i+1) says x_{i+1} â‰¥ 3x_i. To prove R(i+1) from the recurrence x_{i+1} = 6x_i - 8x_{i-1}, you need a bound on x_{i-1} in terms of x_i. R(i) gives x_i â‰¥ 3x_{i-1}, which means x_{i-1} â‰¤ x_i/3. But that gives an upper bound on x_{i-1}, which when you subtract 8x_{i-1} actually helps (larger subtraction = smaller result). Wait, let me think more carefully. x_{i+1} = 6x_i - 8x_{i-1}. We want x_{i+1} â‰¥ 3x_i, i.e., 6x_i - 8x_{i-1} â‰¥ 3x_i, i.e., 3x_i â‰¥ 8x_{i-1}, i.e., x_i â‰¥ (8/3)x_{i-1}. R(i) gives x_i â‰¥ 3x_{i-1} â‰¥ (8/3)x_{i-1}, so actually R(i) IS sufficient! So what IS Erik's actual mistake? Let me re-read the problem. Erik uses regular (not strong) induction. The base case is R(2). But to prove R(i+1) from R(i): R(i) says x_i â‰¥ 3x_{i-1}. We need x_{i+1} â‰¥ 3x_i. x_{i+1} = 6x_i - 8x_{i-1} â‰¥ 6x_i - 8(x_i/3) = 6x_i - (8/3)x_i = (10/3)x_i â‰¥ 3x_i. Wait, that actually works algebraically... So what IS the mistake? The hint says the mistake is using regular induction - but looking again, the issue might be that to apply R(i) to get x_{i-1} â‰¤ x_i/3, we need R(i) which says x_i â‰¥ 3x_{i-1} - but this is fine. Hmm, wait. Actually I think the mistake is more subtle. Regular induction with base case R(2) means we prove R(2) â†’ R(3) â†’ R(4)... But to prove R(3), we assume R(2): x_2 â‰¥ 3x_1. We need x_3 â‰¥ 3x_2. x_3 = 6x_2 - 8x_1. From R(2): x_1 â‰¤ x_2/3. So x_3 = 6x_2 - 8x_1 â‰¥ 6x_2 - 8x_2/3 = (10/3)x_2 â‰¥ 3x_2. This seems to work! So maybe Erik's proof actually goes through? Then what IS the mistake? Let me re-read. 'Erroneous Erik tries to help by proving the claim using (regular) induction on the stronger hypothesis R(i) := xi â‰¥ 3Â·x_{i-1}, for every i â‰¥ 2, with base case R(2): x2 = 13 â‰¥ 12 = 3Â·x1.' The hint for (d) says 'see if you can fix Erroneous Erik's argument.' The issue with Erik is that even if he successfully proves R(i) for all iâ‰¥2, this doesn't directly prove xi > 0 for all iâ‰¥1. He proves xi â‰¥ 3x_{i-1} for iâ‰¥2, but to conclude xi > 0 from this, you'd need x1 > 0 as a base, which... x1 = 4 > 0. Then R(2) gives x2 â‰¥ 3x1 > 0, etc. So it seems like it could work. Hmm. Wait, maybe the actual error is that regular induction isn't sufficient because the inductive step for R(i+1) actually needs BOTH R(i) AND R(i-1) or something similar? Let me reconsider. Actually going back: to prove R(i+1), i.e., x_{i+1} â‰¥ 3x_i, using x_{i+1} = 6x_i - 8x_{i-1}, I need a lower bound on x_i and an upper bound on x_{i-1}. Regular induction gives me R(i): x_i â‰¥ 3x_{i-1}, which gives x_{i-1} â‰¤ x_i/3. This is an upper bound on x_{i-1}. So x_{i+1} = 6x_i - 8x_{i-1} â‰¥ 6x_i - (8/3)x_i = (10/3)x_i â‰¥ 3x_i. So the algebra does work with just R(i). Hmm. So maybe Erik's proof actually works and the 'mistake' is something else, like... he only uses regular induction when strong induction would be cleaner? Or maybe the issue is that R(i) for iâ‰¥2 doesn't cover i=1, so the original claim xi > 0 for i=1 isn't covered? Wait - the original claim is xi > 0 for all iâ‰¥1. Erik proves R(i) for iâ‰¥2, which gives x2 â‰¥ 3x1 = 12 > 0, x3 â‰¥ 3x2 > 0, etc. But x1 > 0 isn't proven by R. However x1 = 4 > 0 is given. So that's not really an error in the induction itself. Actually, I think I need to reconsider more carefully. The problem says he uses 'regular induction.' The recurrence is x_{i+1} = 6x_i - 8x_{i-1}. In the inductive step for R(i+1), using regular induction, you only get to assume R(i), which is x_i â‰¥ 3x_{i-1}. But to bound x_{i-1} from above, you rearrange R(i) to get x_{i-1} â‰¤ x_i/3. This only works if the direction is right - R(i) gives a LOWER bound on x_i, which gives an UPPER bound on x_{i-1}. But wait - do we actually know x_{i-1} â‰¥ 0 to use this? We'd need x_{i-1} > 0 to make the argument valid, which is what we're trying to prove in the first place (circular reasoning?). Hmm but x_{i-1} â‰¤ x_i/3 doesn't require x_{i-1} to be positive - it follows directly from x_i â‰¥ 3x_{i-1}. So there's no circularity issue there. I'm now thinking maybe the problem is simpler: maybe the mistake is just that Erik's inductive step, while perhaps algebraically salvageable, doesn't use the correct approach because R(i+1) = x_{i+1} â‰¥ 3x_i needs the recurrence which involves x_{i-1}, and regular induction doesn't give him any information about x_{i-1} beyond what's implied by R(i). But as shown above, R(i) does give enough. OK I want to look at this from the hint for part (d): 'See if you can fix Erroneous Erik's argument. What stronger inductive hypothesis will you use?' This suggests Erik's approach is close but the hypothesis needs strengthening. The issue must be that R(i) alone doesn't work in the inductive step. Let me try once more very carefully. To prove R(i+1): x_{i+1} â‰¥ 3x_i. x_{i+1} = 6x_i - 8x_{i-1}. We need: 6x_i - 8x_{i-1} â‰¥ 3x_i, i.e., 3x_i â‰¥ 8x_{i-1}. From R(i): x_i â‰¥ 3x_{i-1}. So 3x_i â‰¥ 9x_{i-1}. And 9x_{i-1} â‰¥ 8x_{i-1} requires x_{i-1} â‰¥ 0. But we don't know x_{i-1} â‰¥ 0 from the induction hypothesis! R(i) says x_i â‰¥ 3x_{i-1} but x_{i-1} could be negative (we haven't proven the original claim yet). So: if x_{i-1} < 0, then 9x_{i-1} < 8x_{i-1} (multiplying a negative number, the inequality flips direction). So the step '9x_{i-1} â‰¥ 8x_{i-1}' is only valid if x_{i-1} â‰¥ 0, which is what we're trying to prove! THAT is the error. Erik assumes x_{i-1} â‰¥ 0 implicitly. This is the circular reasoning / gap in the proof. And the fix for part (d) would be to use a stronger hypothesis that also establishes positivity, like Q(i) from Brynmor but with correct base cases, or a two-part hypothesis. So the error is subtle: the inductive step requires x_{i-1} â‰¥ 0 to complete the algebra, but this hasn't been established. I should guide the student toward this without giving it away.

exchanges.student: hmm maybe he also needs \(i\ge 2\) so that \(x_{i-1}\) is actually covered by the claim, and the first step would be from \(R(2)\) to \(R(3)\)? Iâ€™m still not seeing the exact flaw tho.
exchanges.tutor: Think about this specific step: from R(i) you know 3x_i â‰¥ 9x_{i-1}. To conclude 9x_{i-1} â‰¥ 8x_{i-1}, what do you need to know about the sign of x_{i-1}?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still not seeing the core issue. I should nudge them toward the sign of x_{i-1}. The key is: when Erik rearranges 3x_i â‰¥ 8x_{i-1} using x_i â‰¥ 3x_{i-1} to get 9x_{i-1} â‰¥ 8x_{i-1}, this step requires x_{i-1} â‰¥ 0. But that's exactly what hasn't been proven yet. I'll ask a targeted question about the sign.

exchanges.student: ohhh right, that only works if \(x_{i-1}\ge 0\) (or \(>0\)). if \(x_{i-1}\) were negative, multiplying by 9 vs 8 wouldnâ€™t preserve the inequality that way, so Erikâ€™s inductive hypothesis is too weak bc it doesnâ€™t also give positivity.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly â€” R(i) alone doesn't guarantee x_{i-1} â‰¥ 0, so the step is unjustified. That's the gap. Now look at Zach (part c). His hypothesis is S(i): x_i = 4^i. What goes wrong in his very first inductive step, when he tries to prove S(2)?

This is a great insight. I should affirm this and move them to part (c) with Zach, keeping momentum going.

exchanges.student: I think to prove \(S(2)\) from the recurrence heâ€™d need \(x_0\) and \(x_1\), or at least more than just \(S(1)\). so the first inductive step already fails since the recurrence for \(x_{i+1}\) uses two previous terms.

The inductive step tries to prove S(2) from S(1), but S(2) is false, so the inductive step must fail somewhere. This is the precise error.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout all ten turns, never giving away answers directly. Each question built on the student's prior response, scaffolding was well-paced, and the tutor redirected the student productively when confused (e.g., Turn 9 to Turn 10). No submission-ready content was provided, no rewriting occurred, and the dialogue stayed tightly anchored to the assignment. Responses were concise and appropriately targeted.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided direct answers or near-submission-ready reasoning. Every turn posed a targeted question that required the student to do the reasoning work. Key ideas (missing base case, sign of x_{i-1}, falsity of S(2)) were elicited from the student, not stated first by the tutor. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming 'Exactly right' before moving on) and diagnosed confusion before responding (e.g., Turn 6 slowing down to isolate the specific algebraic step). 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, pointing out what kind of information the inductive hypothesis does or does not supply, which is method-level guidance. 2.1: No repetitive cycling; each turn advanced the dialogue. 2.2: The conversation stayed on-task throughout. 3.1: Responses were short and focused, appropriate for interactive tutoring. 3.2: Tone was supportive and formative, with no grading language.
